questions.question: Gold shines brightly. What property does this demonstrate?
Copper can be drawn into wires. What property does this demonstrate?
Aluminium is used in utensils as it is a good conductor of heat. What property does this demonstrate?
An iron nail produces a ringing sound when it falls. What property does this demonstrate?
Lead can be cast into thin sheets. What property does this demonstrate?
Chromium resists indentation by an external force. What property does this demonstrate?
A steel wire can withstand a high longitudinal pull without breaking. What property does this demonstrate?
Name a metal that is liquid at room temperature, non-ductile and has low tensile strength.
Name a metal that is not easily beaten into sheets at relevant temperatures.
Name a metal that floats on water and has a low melting point.
Name a metal used in making packaging foils due to high malleability.
Name a metal that burns brightly and is used in fireworks.
Name a metal that is highly ductile and a good conductor of electricity.
Name a metal with bright lustre and easy malleability.
Name a non-metal that sublimes and is lustrous.
Name a non-metal that shows ability to be drawn into wires/fibres (exception to general rule).
Name a non-metal that allows electric current to flow through it.
Name a non-metal with a comparatively high melting point.
Name a comparatively heavy non-metal.
Name a non-metal with high tensile strength (exception to general rule).
What is observed when a metal sample (A) is hammered?
questions.answer: It flattens into a thin sheet (metals are malleable).

questions.question: What is observed when a sulphur sample (B) is hammered?
questions.answer: It breaks into powder or pieces (non-metals are brittle).

questions.question: Compare the conclusions from experiments on sample A (metal) and sample B (sulphur).
questions.answer: Metals are malleable (can be hammered into sheets), while non-metals are brittle (break into pieces when hammered).

questions.question: How can you differentiate between a metal and a non-metal using a bulb-conductivity setup?
questions.answer: Place the sample in the circuit. If the bulb glows, the sample is a metal (good conductor of electricity). If the bulb does not glow, the sample is a non-metal.

questions.question: If graphite is placed at position X in the bulb circuit, what is the observation? Give a reason.
questions.answer: The bulb glows because graphite is a form of carbon that conducts electricity (it is an exception among non-metals).

questions.question: What is the outer shell of an atom called?
Metals generally have how many electrons in their outer shell?
Non-metals generally have how many electrons in their outer shell?
Metals lose electrons and form what type of ions?
Non-metals gain electrons and form what type of ions?
Metals react with dilute acids to produce which gas?
Name two metals that do not react with water/steam and dilute acids.
Name two metals that do not form oxides easily with air/oxygen.
Which is the most reactive metal in the activity series?
Name the two least reactive metals in the activity series.
Reactive metals are placed at the extreme left or right of the modern periodic table?
Weak metals are placed at the right, left, or middle of the periodic table?
Non-metals are placed in the lower right or upper right corner of the periodic table?
Noble gases are in Group 18. How many noble gases are there? Are they metals or non-metals?
questions.answer: There are six noble gases. They are classified as non-metals.

questions.question: Halogens are in which group of the periodic table? Give examples.
questions.answer: Group 17. Examples: Fluorine (F), Chlorine (Cl), Bromine (Br), Iodine (I)

questions.question: Name a base metal which gets easily oxidized.
Name a noble metal other than gold.
Name a heavy metal other than lead.
Name a monoatomic inert non-metal used in light bulbs.
Name a diatomic non-metal added to swimming pools to kill germs.
Name a halogen that is solid at room temperature.
Name a polyatomic non-metal other than phosphorus.
Name the gaseous non-metal that is the major component of the atmosphere.
Name a solid non-metal used in lead pencils.
Name the most reactive diatomic halogen.
Name the potassium salt of bromine.
Name the gaseous diatomic halogen related to prevention of tooth decay.
questions.answer: Fluorine (used as fluoride compounds in water treatment and toothpaste)

questions.question: Elements showing properties of both metals and non-metals are called what?
Metalloids generally show characteristics of what?
Name a metalloid which is lustrous but not malleable or ductile.
Are metalloids generally hard or brittle?
Which metalloid (compound) is applied on burn patients?
Name a metalloid used as a fire retardant.
Which class of elements is used as semiconductors in cell phones and computers?
Why does rusting take place only in the presence of moisture and oxygen?
questions.answer: Rusting is the oxidation of iron, which requires both water (moisture) and oxygen. In the absence of either, rusting does not occur.

questions.question: Why is rusting of iron more detrimental than the tarnishing of copper?
questions.answer: Rust (iron oxide) is porous and flaky — it does not form a protective layer. It keeps exposing fresh iron to moisture and oxygen, so corrosion continues. Copper tarnish (patina) forms a thin protective layer that prevents further corrosion.

questions.question: In the rusting experiment, why is anhydrous calcium chloride kept in test tube A?
questions.answer: To absorb moisture (water vapour) from the air, creating a dry environment to show that iron does not rust without moisture.

questions.question: In the rusting experiment, why is boiled water covered with oil used in test tube B?
questions.answer: Boiling removes dissolved oxygen from the water, and the oil layer prevents air (and oxygen) from entering. This shows that iron does not rust without oxygen.

questions.question: In the rusting experiment, why does the iron nail in test tube C (ordinary tap water) rust?
questions.answer: Because ordinary tap water provides both moisture and dissolved oxygen — the two conditions required for rusting.

questions.question: Why is a thin oil layer applied on moving machinery parts?
questions.answer: Oil prevents contact between the iron/steel surface and air/moisture, thus preventing rusting. It also acts as a lubricant.

questions.question: Why do street lights and telegraph poles have an aluminium paint coat?
questions.answer: Aluminium paint forms a protective anti-corrosion coating that prevents the iron from coming in contact with moisture and oxygen.

questions.question: Why is coal tar or pitch used on the lower part of ships?
questions.answer: The lower part of ships is constantly in contact with water. Coal tar/pitch provides a strong waterproof protective coating to prevent corrosion.

questions.question: Why do cooking vessels have enamel coating and not paint coating?
questions.answer: Enamel is heat-resistant and non-reactive with food. Paint would burn, peel off and could contaminate food when heated.

questions.question: Why is galvanization used on articles exposed to moisture, such as buckets?
questions.answer: Galvanization coats iron with a layer of zinc, which prevents rusting even when scratched (zinc corrodes preferentially, protecting the iron).

questions.question: Why are food containers not made of galvanized steel?
questions.answer: Zinc may react with food acids, producing toxic zinc compounds that could contaminate the food.

questions.question: Why is chrome plating used mostly for decorative articles?
questions.answer: Chrome plating gives a bright, shiny, corrosion-resistant finish that looks attractive, but it is costlier than other methods like galvanization.

questions.question: Why does a rust suspension turn moist red litmus blue?
questions.answer: Because rust (hydrated iron oxide) is basic in nature.

questions.question: Why does a green deposit form on copper only in moist atmosphere?
questions.answer: In moist atmosphere, copper reacts with carbon dioxide (CO₂), moisture (H₂O) and oxygen (O₂) to form basic copper carbonate — which is the green deposit (patina).

questions.question: Why has the iron pillar at Qutub Minar not rusted easily over centuries?
questions.answer: The iron pillar has a unique corrosion-resistant composition — it contains a high percentage of phosphorus which forms a thin protective passive film on the surface, preventing further rusting.

questions.question: Which metal is used for making bullets? Why?
questions.answer: Lead — because it is dense and soft, making it easy to shape into bullets.

questions.question: Which metal is used for dry cells? Why?
questions.answer: Zinc — it acts as the container (anode) and reacts suitably in the electrochemical reaction of the dry cell.

questions.question: Which metal/alloy is used for automobiles and ships? Why?
questions.answer: Steel (alloy of iron) — because it has high tensile strength and durability.

questions.question: Which metal is used for packaging and paints? Why?
questions.answer: Aluminium — because it is highly malleable (can be made into thin foils) and forms a protective oxide layer.

questions.question: Which metal is used for cables and calorimeters? Why?
questions.answer: Copper — because it has very high electrical conductivity (for cables) and thermal conductivity (for calorimeters).

questions.question: Which metal is used in photography (flash)? Why?
questions.answer: Magnesium — because it burns with an intense bright white light.

questions.question: Which metal is used for galvanizing? Why?
questions.answer: Zinc — because its coating protects iron from rusting (galvanization).

questions.question: Which metal is used for flexible pipes? Why?
questions.answer: Lead — because it is soft and malleable, allowing it to be bent into flexible shapes.

questions.question: Which metal is used for gutter covers and weights? Why?
questions.answer: Pig iron (cast iron) — because it is heavy and hard, suitable for covers and weights.

questions.question: Which metal is used in fireworks? Why?
questions.answer: Magnesium — because it burns with an intense bright white flame.
